burial.plot_notes: FindAGrave #139501351. Pine Ridge Cemetery Saranac Lake NY.

parents.father_research_notes: Father: Rev. Edward Newman Packard D.D. (b.c.1840s, New England minister). Family lived in Evanston IL c.1872-1876, then Dorchester MA. Siblings: Charlotte Mellen Packard (b.1872, d.1954); Elizabeth Ford Packard Quereau (b.1876, d.1948), whose son Edmund Packard Quereau lived to 98; Agnes Kent Packard Cooper (b.1881, d.1963), who served as president of New York Women's Board of Missions 1921-22 and was a twenty-year member of American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions. Agnes's son Lawrence Carleton Cooper (Michigan Phi Beta Kappa MBA 1932) was a Bankers Trust VP for utility investments. Lawrence's daughter Carol Packard Cooper (b.1941, d.2016) was one of the country's first open heart surgery patients, operated on by Dr. Denton Cooley in 1959 aged 17. Agnes's husband Dr. Hermon Charles Cooper earned a chemistry PhD at Heidelberg 1900 and co-authored an inorganic chemistry textbook with Dutch chemist Arnold Holleman in 1901; he was also a research associate at MIT.

military_service.notes: Served two years during WWI. Specific rank not documented.

military_service.unit: Fort Dix and Holloran General Hospitals
military_service.notes: Medical chief of Fort Dix and Holloran General Hospitals, combined 6,500 beds. Received Legion of Merit for service.

narrative_blocks.header: Commanded 6,500-bed WWII hospitals; received Legion of Merit
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Packard served as a physician in WWI and then from 1941 to 1946 as lieutenant colonel and then colonel commanding 6,500 combined beds at Fort Dix and Holloran General Hospitals — for which he received the Legion of Merit. He married Mary Bissell Betts, 'one of the earliest occupational therapists,' and after her 1926 death continued his medical career, practicing internal medicine and cardiology in Pensacola until 1968.

narrative_blocks.header: Minister's son; sister led American missions board
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Packard came from a distinguished New England clerical family — his father was a D.D. minister and his sister Agnes Cooper served as president of the New York branch of the Women's Board of Missions and held a twenty-year membership on the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions. A niece, Carol Packard Cooper, was among the country's first open heart surgery patients, operated on by Dr. Denton Cooley in 1959 at age seventeen.

burial.plot_notes: FindAGrave #139501372. Pine Ridge Cemetery Saranac Lake NY.

narrative_blocks.header: Pioneer therapist who died at Saranac Lake, thirty-eight
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Bissell Betts married Col. Edward Packard, a physician who would receive the Legion of Merit for commanding 6,500-bed military hospitals in WWII. His 1968 obituary described her as 'one of the earliest occupational therapists' — she died in 1926 at thirty-eight in Saranac Lake, New York, the famous tuberculosis sanatorium town where many sought or provided care during this era.

military_service.notes: Veteran per obituary. Specific WWII service not detailed.
Naval flight surgeon. Completed military service 1954.

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale University graduate; went on to Harvard Medical School and a distinguished career in cardiology and public health.
Harvard Medical School graduate; interned Columbia-Presbyterian; resident Peter Bent Brigham Hospital.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale, Harvard Medical, Korean flight surgeon
narrative_blocks.body: John Mallory Packard graduated from Yale University and Harvard Medical School, interned at Columbia-Presbyterian, and completed military service as a naval flight surgeon in the Korean War before beginning private practice in cardiology and internal medicine in Pensacola in 1954. He went on to co-found the College of Community Health Services at the University of Alabama, served as president of the Alabama Heart Association and Birmingham Academy of Medicine, and was active in genealogical societies — a member of the New England Historic Genealogical Society and the Sons of the American Revolution.
